subject: 英語
question:
次の日記を読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
March 8th (Saturday)

Today I volunteered at the Riverside Public Library's annual book fair. I had signed up two weeks ago after seeing a notice on the library's website. When I arrived at 9 a.m., the head librarian, Ms. Carter, explained my role: I was in charge of sorting donated books by category and helping young visitors find titles they might enjoy.

Around lunchtime, a small girl named Lily came to the children's section looking for a book about dinosaurs. I went through three separate boxes before finding exactly what she needed — a large picture book with colorful illustrations of prehistoric animals. When I handed it to her, her face lit up instantly.

By closing time at 4 p.m., we had helped more than 150 visitors and sold over 200 books. Ms. Carter announced that all the money raised would go toward buying new books for the library's collection. I went home tired but deeply satisfied.
---

choices.label: D

subject: 英語
question:
次のブログを読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
My First Cooking Adventure

Last Sunday, I decided to cook pasta for dinner to surprise my mother, who had to work overtime that day. I had watched a cooking tutorial online the night before and felt confident.

I started by boiling water and cooking the pasta — that part went smoothly. The trouble came when I tried to make tomato sauce. I added far too much salt and forgot to watch the vegetables, which burned on the bottom of the pan. The smell was awful.

I was ready to order takeout when my younger brother, Ryan, walked into the kitchen.

"It smells interesting," he said with a laugh. "Can I help?"

Together, we threw away the ruined sauce and started fresh. Ryan remembered that adding a little sugar can reduce excess saltiness. The new sauce turned out much better. When our mother finally came home and tasted the pasta, she said it was "not bad at all."

That day, I realized that the most important skill in cooking is knowing how to recover from mistakes.
---

語り手がその日に料理をしようと決めた理由は何か。

choices.label: A
choices.text: 賞味期限が近い野菜を使い切りたかったから

choices.label: B
choices.text: オンラインで見た料理の動画の技術を試したかったから

choices.label: C
choices.text: 空腹だったRyanを早く食べさせたかったから

choices.label: D
choices.text: 残業していた母親を驚かせたかったから

subject: 英語
question:
次の日記を読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
June 5th

Today was my first day in Greenfield City. We moved here from Maplewood last weekend because my father got a job promotion that required relocation. Our new apartment is on the eighth floor of a modern building — nothing like our old house with its garden.

I spent most of the morning unpacking boxes. In the afternoon, I took a walk around the neighborhood by myself. The streets are busy and loud, and I got lost twice trying to find the convenience store. I finally asked a lady walking her dog for directions. She was very friendly and even recommended a good cafe nearby.

June 8th

I started at my new school today. I was nervous because I didn't know anyone. During lunch, a girl named Priya sat next to me and introduced herself. She said she had also moved here from another city two years ago, so she understood how I felt. We talked for most of the lunch break.

I'm still not sure I like city life, but today felt a little more manageable.
---

choices.label: B
choices.text: 母親が新しい仕事の機会を見つけたから

choices.label: C
choices.text: 父親が昇進し、転勤が必要になったから

choices.label: D
choices.text: 家族が都会での生活を経験したかったから

次の日記を読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
October 12th

My piano recital is in two weeks, and I can barely play through my piece without stopping. Every time I make a mistake, I feel like everything falls apart. My teacher, Mr. Nakamura, says I am technically ready, but I freeze up the moment I think about the audience.

October 20th

I practiced for three hours today. Something clicked. I stopped trying to play perfectly and just tried to enjoy the music. After practice, I told my mother how nervous I still was. She reminded me of the school play I performed in last year. I had forgotten all my lines at first, but I recovered and received a standing ovation. She said, "You've been here before."

October 27th (Recital Day)

My hands were shaking when I walked out. But as soon as I heard the opening notes I had played so many times, something shifted. I played the entire piece without stopping. Afterward, Mr. Nakamura told me I played with "real feeling."

I used to think performing meant playing without mistakes. Now I know it means connecting with the music.
---

choices.label: D

question:
次のエッセイを読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
Speaking Up: My Story

In my second year of high school, our teacher announced that everyone in the class had to give a five-minute speech. I felt sick the moment I heard it. I had always avoided speaking in front of groups. Just the thought of everyone watching me made my heart race.

For two weeks, I practiced alone in my room. I memorized every word of my speech about ocean conservation. But when the day came, I stood up and my mind went blank. I stared at my paper without saying a word for what felt like minutes.

Then something unexpected happened. My best friend Lisa called out from the back: "Go, Tom!" A few others joined in. Suddenly, the silence was broken, and I found my voice. I didn't stick to the memorized script — I just talked about why protecting the ocean mattered to me.

After class, several classmates said they had never thought about ocean pollution that way. My teacher wrote on my evaluation, "Spoke from the heart."

I still get nervous before speaking in public. But now I know that nervousness doesn't mean I can't do it.
---

Tomが先生の発表を聞いてすぐに気分が悪くなった理由は何か。

choices.label: A
choices.text: スピーチの準備が何もできていなかったから

choices.label: B
choices.text: 5分間のスピーチは長すぎると感じたから

choices.label: C
choices.text: もともと人前で話すことを避けてきたから

choices.label: D
choices.text: スピーチのテーマがすでに決められていたから

subject: 英語
question:
次の日記を読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
April 3rd

My new neighbor, Mr. Yamamoto, moved in last week. He is around 75 years old and lives alone. This morning he knocked on our door to return a package that had been delivered to his address by mistake. He seemed a little unsure of himself and left quickly.

April 18th

I ran into Mr. Yamamoto in the hallway and asked if he needed anything from the supermarket since I was going. He looked surprised, then smiled and asked for a small bag of rice. When I returned with the rice, he invited me in for tea. He told me he had worked as a photographer for 30 years and showed me prints of landscapes from all over Japan.

May 7th

Mr. Yamamoto came to our door again today — this time with a jar of homemade jam as a gift. He said talking with someone had made a big difference in his week. I realized I had assumed that older people preferred to be left alone. I was completely wrong about that.
---

語り手とYamamotoさんが最初に出会ったのはどのような状況か。

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yamamotoさんが誤配達された荷物を届けに来た

choices.label: C
choices.text: 語り手がYamamotoさんのドアをノックして歓迎した

choices.label: D

choices.label: A
choices.text: これまで暮らしたさまざまな都市の話

choices.label: B

subject: 英語
question:
次の日記を読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
April 3rd

My new neighbor, Mr. Yamamoto, moved in last week. He is around 75 years old and lives alone. This morning he knocked on our door to return a package that had been delivered to his address by mistake. He seemed a little unsure of himself and left quickly.

April 18th

I ran into Mr. Yamamoto in the hallway and asked if he needed anything from the supermarket since I was going. He looked surprised, then smiled and asked for a small bag of rice. When I returned with the rice, he invited me in for tea. He told me he had worked as a photographer for 30 years and showed me prints of landscapes from all over Japan.

May 7th

Mr. Yamamoto came to our door again today — this time with a jar of homemade jam as a gift. He said talking with someone had made a big difference in his week. I realized I had assumed that older people preferred to be left alone. I was completely wrong about that.
---

語り手が誤っていたと気づいた思い込みは何か。

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yamamotoさんは若い隣人との交流を望まないだろうという思い込み

choices.label: B
choices.text: ひとりで暮らしている人はいつも不幸だという思い込み

choices.label: C
choices.text: 最初は人見知りの人は親しくなりにくいという思い込み

choices.label: D
choices.text: 高齢者は一人でいることを好むという思い込み

subject: 英語
question:
次の日記を読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
What Failing Taught Me

I failed my university entrance exam in February. I had studied for over a year, given up social events, slept only six hours a night, and still came up short. The day I saw my results, I locked myself in my room and refused to talk to anyone.

For a week, I replayed every decision I had made: the chapters I had skimmed, the mock tests I had skipped, the morning practices I had delayed. I was convinced I had wasted an entire year.

Then, slowly, I started to see things differently. I had, in fact, become a better reader, a stronger problem-solver, and more disciplined than I had ever been. The skills I had built were real — they did not disappear just because my score was not high enough.

I applied to a different university and began attending the following spring. In my first month, I consistently scored among the top students in my class. A classmate who knew about my exam failure once asked how I felt about that year of studying. "It was the best preparation I could have had," I told him. And I meant it.

Failure, I now believe, is not the opposite of progress. It is often a part of it.
---

この日記を通じて、語り手が最も伝えたいことは何か。

choices.label: A
choices.text: 失敗は個人の成長の重要な一部であることがある

choices.label: B
choices.text: 他の人よりも一生懸命に勉強することが学業成功の鍵だ

choices.label: C
choices.text: 大学入学試験の仕組みは改善が必要だ

choices.label: D
choices.text: 大学に入る前に1年間休むことは有益だ

subject: 英語
question:
次の日記を読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
Coming Home

Ten years had passed since I left Harborview, the small coastal town where I grew up. I returned last summer for my grandmother's 90th birthday party. I expected to feel nostalgic, perhaps a little sentimental. What I did not expect was to feel like a stranger.

The bakery where I used to buy bread every Saturday was now a clothing store. The old wooden pier had been rebuilt in concrete. Even the air smelled different — less like salt and seaweed, more like exhaust fumes from the new highway nearby.

At the party, I talked with relatives I had not seen in years. My cousin Kento, who still lives in Harborview, said, "It must be strange to see how much has changed." I nodded. But the more I thought about it, the more I realized that I had changed just as much as the town had.

The Harborview I remembered was, in part, a place I had imagined — one shaped by the emotions and perspective of a much younger version of myself. The real town had always been changing; I simply had not been watching.

Coming home reminded me that memory is not a record of the past. It is a story we tell ourselves, shaped by who we were at the time.
---

この日記で語り手が最も伝えたいことは何か。

choices.label: A
choices.text: 記憶は過去の正確な記録ではなく、個人的な解釈だ

choices.label: B
choices.text: 故郷の人間関係は他の場所で結ばれた関係よりいつも大切だ

choices.label: C
choices.text: 故郷に帰ることは深く失望させられる体験になりうる

choices.label: D
choices.text: 小さな町は新たな開発によって大都市より速く変わる

subject: 英語
question:
次のブログを読んで、設問に答えなさい。

---
The Painting That Taught Me to Let Go

For my school's art festival, I had planned to create a large oil painting of Mount Fuji at sunrise. I had sketched the outline, chosen the colors, and imagined exactly how it would look. I was going to make something perfect.

Three days before the deadline, I dropped the half-finished canvas while moving it to a better light. It landed face-first on the floor. When I turned it over, the wet paint had smeared in all directions, mixing the oranges and blues into an unexpected swirling pattern.

I stood there for a moment, devastated. Then, slowly, I tilted the canvas and looked again. The smeared paint looked almost like a storm over the ocean, dynamic and alive in a way that my careful brushstrokes never were.

I decided not to try to restore the original plan. Instead, I built on what the accident had created, adding layers of color to deepen the stormy feeling. I gave the finished work a new title: "Unexpected Horizon."

At the festival, a teacher who judges our work told me it was "the most emotionally expressive piece" she had seen from a student in years.

The painting I had planned to make was about Mount Fuji. The painting I made was about letting go.
---

締め切り3日前に語り手のキャンバスに何が起きたか。

choices.label: A

choices.label: B

choices.label: C
choices.text: 語り手が元のデザインを塗りつぶして最初からやり直した

choices.label: D
choices.text: 落下して、濡れた絵の具が床についてにじんだ
